summary:
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, heir presumptive to the Austro-Hungarian throne, on 28 June 1914 in Sarajevo is widely regarded as the immediate spark that ignited [[event:110|World War I]]. Franz Ferdinand and his wife Sophie were shot by Gavrilo Princip, a Bosnian Serb nationalist affiliated with the secret society Ujedinjenje ili smrt (Union or Death, known as the Black Hand). The killing took place during an official visit to Bosnia, which Austria-Hungary had annexed in 1908 — a move bitterly resented by Serbian nationalists who dreamed of uniting the South Slav peoples.

The assassination triggered a rapid escalation through Europe's interlocking alliance system. Austria-Hungary, backed by Germany's infamous "blank check" of unconditional support, issued a harsh ultimatum to Serbia. When Serbia's response was deemed insufficient, Austria-Hungary declared war on 28 July 1914. Russia began mobilizing to support Serbia; Germany declared war on Russia and then France; and when Germany violated Belgian neutrality by marching toward France, Britain entered the war. Within six weeks, most of Europe's great powers were at war.

Historians debate whether the assassination was truly the cause of the war or merely the trigger for a conflict that would have erupted through some other mechanism. The underlying causes — militarism, imperial rivalry, nationalist tensions in the Balkans, and the dangerously rigid alliance systems — had been building for decades. The [[event:3444|Balkan Wars]] of 1912–13 had already demonstrated the region's instability. In this view, Franz Ferdinand's death was the spark that fell into a powder keg that European statesmen and military planners had collectively constructed.
